title: About
text:
These pages contain the code for the analysis and generation of figures discussed in our recent manuscript which can be referred to in this link.


Rotem Aharon1,2, Alicia Oshlack1,2,3




Computational Biology Program, Peter MacCallum Cancer Centre, Parkville, VIC, Australia
Sir Peter MacCallum Department of Oncology, University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC, Australia
School of Mathematics and Statistics, University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC, Australia




Previously, RNA sequencing required Reverse Transcription (RT) and PCR amplification. PCR amplification is known to induce biases associated with expression levels, transcript lengths and GC content. Biases introduced by RT have been described more recently, and their quantification has been more elusive. Oxford Nanopore Technology (ONT) enables the direct sequencing of RNA without RT, and also the sequencing of cDNA without PCR amplification. This allows us to gain further insights into how different aspects of RNA preparation bias the data, with a new focus on isoforms structure and abundance.
Using seven cell line data sets from SG-NEx, we compare nanopore RNA sequencing of direct RNA (dRNA), direct cDNA (dcDNA) and PCR amplified cDNA (PCRcDNA). Across all the cell lines we identify hundreds of Differentially Expressed (DE) genes and isoforms, indicating a significant change in observed expression between the protocols. Furthermore, we identify Differential Transcript Usage (DTU) between protocols in hundreds of genes across cell lines, indicating significant changes in the measured isoform proportions.
Analysis of DE genes and isoforms in the context of expression levels, sequence length and GC content shows that, as expected, the largest number of DE isoforms is between the PCR samples and non-PCR samples. DE isoforms display known amplification bias with respect to expression level, sequence length and GC content. Interestingly we also observe decreased counts after PCR associated with higher levels of the m6A methylation mark on the native RNA.
We also analyse structural differences between isoforms that switch in dominance between direct RNA and PCR cDNA samples, and find that while some switching events relate to simple fragmentation/truncation of the RNA, most involve more complex structural differences including novel junctions, such as exon truncation, exon skipping and intron retention. When compared to differences found between background transcripts selected from non DTU genes, inserted introns (exitrons) are significantly more common in the isoforms after RT and amplification. Last, we find that visualisation of raw coverage data is a crucial step in the qualitative validation of experimental findings and should be included to validate analysis results.



For attribution, please cite this work as:

title: About
text:
Computational Biology Program, Peter MacCallum Cancer Centre, Parkville, VIC, Australia
Sir Peter MacCallum Department of Oncology, University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC, Australia
School of Mathematics and Statistics, University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC, Australia

title: About
text:
Previously, RNA sequencing required Reverse Transcription (RT) and PCR amplification. PCR amplification is known to induce biases associated with expression levels, transcript lengths and GC content. Biases introduced by RT have been described more recently, and their quantification has been more elusive. Oxford Nanopore Technology (ONT) enables the direct sequencing of RNA without RT, and also the sequencing of cDNA without PCR amplification. This allows us to gain further insights into how different aspects of RNA preparation bias the data, with a new focus on isoforms structure and abundance.
Using seven cell line data sets from SG-NEx, we compare nanopore RNA sequencing of direct RNA (dRNA), direct cDNA (dcDNA) and PCR amplified cDNA (PCRcDNA). Across all the cell lines we identify hundreds of Differentially Expressed (DE) genes and isoforms, indicating a significant change in observed expression between the protocols. Furthermore, we identify Differential Transcript Usage (DTU) between protocols in hundreds of genes across cell lines, indicating significant changes in the measured isoform proportions.
Analysis of DE genes and isoforms in the context of expression levels, sequence length and GC content shows that, as expected, the largest number of DE isoforms is between the PCR samples and non-PCR samples. DE isoforms display known amplification bias with respect to expression level, sequence length and GC content. Interestingly we also observe decreased counts after PCR associated with higher levels of the m6A methylation mark on the native RNA.
We also analyse structural differences between isoforms that switch in dominance between direct RNA and PCR cDNA samples, and find that while some switching events relate to simple fragmentation/truncation of the RNA, most involve more complex structural differences including novel junctions, such as exon truncation, exon skipping and intron retention. When compared to differences found between background transcripts selected from non DTU genes, inserted introns (exitrons) are significantly more common in the isoforms after RT and amplification. Last, we find that visualisation of raw coverage data is a crucial step in the qualitative validation of experimental findings and should be included to validate analysis results.

title: About
text: For attribution, please cite this work as:

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
text: The data analysed here is publicly available from the SG-Nex database (Ying Chen et al. 2025). The SG-NEx data was accessed in June 2025 registry.opendata.aws/sg-nex-data. We started by downloading 107 .bam files (samples), which include read alignment to the human genome (hg38) for each of the replicates in each of the cell lines. ‘bambu’ v3.8.3 with NDR=0.1 was used to identify and quantify the genes and transcripts in all of the samples together. This resulted in a SummarisedExperiment R object, which includes transcript counts, and full length read counts as separate assays. Gene counts were obtained by using bambu::transcriptToGeneExpression, which effectively adds the transcript counts for each gene. For each sample, library size was obtained by adding up all transcript counts. Samples with very low or very high library size <400,000 or >40,000,000 were excluded, as well as those that had full length to all transcript ratio lower than 0.2. With the remaining 79 samples, MDS dimensional reduction plots were generated using edgeR::plotMDS for each of the assays independently, with top=2000, to account for the fact transcript counts are not independent of each other.

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
section: 1.1 Library size based on Transcript counts
text:
1.1 Library size based on Transcript counts


Code
libsize_low_lim = 4e+05

libsize_high_lim = 4e+07
all_colData <- data.frame(colData(analysis_se_no_ev))

# plot all library sizes in a boxplot
f1a <- all_colData %>%
    ggplot(aes(cellType, log2(counts_libsize), color = protocol)) + geom_boxplot() +
    geom_jitter(shape = 16, position = position_dodge(width = 0.75)) + geom_hline(yintercept = log2(libsize_low_lim)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = log2(libsize_high_lim)) + labs(x = "", y = "log2[library size (all transcript counts)") +
    theme(legend.position = "none")

f1a









Adjust plot limits for better representation of variability between samples


Code
f1a <- f1a + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(14, 26.5))
f1a

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
section: 1.2 Full length to all count library size ratios
text:
1.2 Full length to all count library size ratios


Code
cell_line_colours <- RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(9, "Oranges")
# hex <- hue_pal()(6)
named_cell_line_colours <- list(A549 = cell_line_colours[3], H9 = cell_line_colours[4],
    HEYA8 = cell_line_colours[5], HepG2 = cell_line_colours[6], Hct116 = cell_line_colours[7],
    K562 = cell_line_colours[8], MCF7 = cell_line_colours[9])



p2 <- all_colData %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = log2(counts_libsize), y = fullLengthCounts_libsize/counts_libsize,
        color = cellType, shape = protocol)) + scale_shape_manual(values = c(16,
    17, 4, 15)) + geom_point(, size = 3, stroke = 1) + geom_vline(xintercept = log2(libsize_low_lim)) +
    annotate(geom = "text", x = log2(libsize_low_lim - 50000), y = 0.55, label = "Library size lower limit",
        angle = "90") + geom_hline(yintercept = 0.2) + annotate(geom = "text", x = 23,
    y = 0.22, label = "Full-length / all transcripts = 0.2", angle = "0") + theme(legend.position = "none") +
    scale_color_manual(values = named_cell_line_colours) + labs(x = "Library size (log2, all transcript counts)",
    y = "Library size ratio \n (Full length transcripts)/(all transcripts)")

p2









Adjust plot limits for better representation of variability between samples


Code
f1b <- p2 + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(16, 25))
f1b

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
text:
2.1 Figure 1: Data overview


Code
numPlots <- ggarrange(f1a, f1b, labels = "auto", common.legend = TRUE, legend = "left",
    ncol = 2, nrow = 1)


pdf("allDataOverview_with_pacbio.pdf", width = 12, height = 12)
dataOverview <- ggarrange(numPlots, MDSplots, nrow = 2, heights = c(1.2, 3))
dataOverview
invisible(dev.off())


png("allDataOverview_with_pacbio.png", width = 30, height = 30, units = "cm", bg = "white",
    res = 300)
dataOverview <- ggarrange(numPlots, MDSplots, nrow = 2, heights = c(1.2, 3))

dataOverview
invisible(dev.off())











Figure 1: a) Library size (number of mapped reads) in each sample, across preparation protocols and cell lines. The samples between the horizontal lines were retained for analysis. b) Fraction of full length transcript reads as a function of log2(library size). MDS plots of gene counts (c,d), total transcript counts (e,f) and full length transcript counts (g,h). Samples separate based on cell line (colour) but also on preparation protocol (shape) especially at the isoform level.












Note



The total number of samples quantified by bambu is 105.
After filtering out samples based on library size (only include 4^{5} < transcripts library size < 4^{7}), there are 77 samples included in the MDS plots.

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
text:
3.1 Samples list and exlusion criteria

3.1.1 Create table


Code
downloaded_samples_table <- data.frame(colData(analysis_se)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(sample_name = name, library_size = counts_libsize, cell_line = cellType,
        grouped_protocol = protocol) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(protocol = raw_protocol) %>%
    select(-c(raw_protocol, cellType, group, gene_counts_libsize))



downloaded_samples_table$in_1a_b <- downloaded_samples_table$cell_line != "MCF7-EV"
downloaded_samples_table$in_1a_b <- ifelse(downloaded_samples_table$in_1a_b, "Yes",
    "No")



downloaded_samples_table$exluded_from_1c_h <- downloaded_samples_table$cell_line ==
    "MCF7-EV" | downloaded_samples_table$counts_libsize < libsize_low_lim | downloaded_samples_table$counts_libsize >
    libsize_high_lim | downloaded_samples_table$fullLengthCounts_libsize/downloaded_samples_table$counts_libsize <
    0.2
downloaded_samples_table$in_1c_h <- ifelse(downloaded_samples_table$exluded_from_1c_h,
    "No", "Yes")




Code
selected_samples_ont_only_se_path <- "../../data/analysis_output/ont_only_counts/selected_samples.rds"

ont_selected_samples_se <- readRDS(selected_samples_ont_only_se_path)


downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1a_b <- downloaded_samples_table$grouped_protocol !=
    "PacBio-SMRTcell" & downloaded_samples_table$cell_line != "MCF7-EV"

downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1a_b <- ifelse(downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1a_b,
    "Yes", "No")


downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1c_h <- downloaded_samples_table$name %in% colData(ont_selected_samples_se)$name




downloaded_samples_table$exluded_from_DTU <- !downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1c_h |
    all_colData$protocol == "PacBio-SMRTcell" | all_colData$protocol == "directcDNA"


downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1c_h <- ifelse(downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1c_h,
    "Yes", "No")
downloaded_samples_table$in_DE_fig2 <- downloaded_samples_table$in_sup1c_h
downloaded_samples_table$in_DTU_fig3 <- ifelse(downloaded_samples_table$exluded_from_DTU,
    "No", "Yes")


downloaded_samples_table <- downloaded_samples_table %>%
    select(-c(name, sample_name, exluded_from_1c_h, exluded_from_DTU, counts_libsize,
        fullLengthCounts_libsize)) %>%
    relocate(c(protocol, grouped_protocol), .before = cell_line)  # %>% relocate(c(protocol,grouped_protocol), .before = cell_line)


downloaded_samples_table <- downloaded_samples_table[order(downloaded_samples_table$cell_line,
    downloaded_samples_table$protocol), ]

# Count number of samples included in each column
yes_counts <- colSums(downloaded_samples_table == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE)

# Append to the bottom of the data frame
downloaded_samples_table <- rbind(downloaded_samples_table, yes_counts)

# Give the new row a clear row name
rownames(downloaded_samples_table)[nrow(downloaded_samples_table)] <- "Total"




Code
knitr::kable(x = downloaded_samples_table, caption = paste0("Sample inclusion table"))



Sample inclusion table

title: 1 Data overview PacBio samples included  (Figure 1)
section: 3.2 Auto format table to pdf (using latex)
text:
3.2 Auto format table to pdf (using latex)


Code
latex_table <- kable(downloaded_samples_table, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE,
    longtable = TRUE, escape = TRUE, caption = "Sample inclusion table", align = rep("c",
        ncol(downloaded_samples_table))) %>%
    kable_styling(latex_options = c("striped", "hold_position", "repeat_header"),
        font_size = 6, full_width = FALSE, position = "center")

# Use landscape geometry instead of kableExtra's landscape()
latex_doc <- paste0("\\documentclass{article}\n", "\\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}\n",
    "\\usepackage{booktabs}\n", "\\usepackage{longtable}\n", "\\usepackage{array}\n",
    "\\usepackage{colortbl}\n", "\\usepackage{xcolor}\n", "\\usepackage[landscape, margin=0.5in]{geometry}\n",
    "\\begin{document}\n", latex_table, "\n\\end{document}")

# Write LaTeX file
writeLines(latex_doc, "table1.tex")

# Compile to PDF (requires tinytex or LaTeX installation)
tinytex::pdflatex("table1.tex")


[1] "table1.pdf"





Table 1: Sample inclusion table
